target_question: Why do arrays start at 0 instead of 1?
description: Why array indices start at 0 in most programming languages
initial_misconception: Arrays should start at 1 because that's more natural for counting.
initial_student_statement: Why does the first element have index 0? That's confusing. When I count things, I start at 1, so arrays should too.
correct_understanding: Array indices represent offsets from the start of the array in memory. The first element is at offset 0 from the base address. Starting at 0 also makes arithmetic with indices simpler and avoids off-by-one errors in many algorithms.
good_question_templates: Where in the computer's memory do you think an array lives?
If arrays are stored contiguously in memory, how would you describe the position of the first element?
What's the distance from the start of the array to the first element?
Think of a ruler — what number is at the very starting point?
What would happen to index calculations if we started at 1 instead?

confused: I don't know, somewhere in memory? I just know the first thing should be number 1.
Why does it matter where it's stored? 1 just makes more sense for humans.
uncertain: Hmm, so the array takes up space in memory? Like consecutive slots?
Wait, so the index is about where things are in memory, not just counting?
starting_to_see: Oh, so the first element is right at the beginning — it has zero distance from the start!
So the index is more like an offset... and the first item has an offset of 0 from the start.
almost_there: That makes sense! It's not 'which number item,' it's 'how far from the beginning.' And the first one IS the beginning, so it's 0!
I see — 0-indexing aligns with how memory works. Starting at 1 would always require subtracting 1 internally.
understood: I get it! Array indices are memory offsets from the base address. The first element IS the base, so its offset is 0. Starting at 0 makes pointer arithmetic clean and avoids off-by-one issues.
Now I understand — it's not about human counting, it's about computer memory layout. 0-indexing is simpler for the machine and actually prevents bugs in index math.
